turns.content: I'm trying to parallelize a data processing task in the browser and I thought using web workers from Rust would be my best bet. I read somewhere that wasm-bindgen-rayon makes this easy, so I assumed I could just spawn a thread inside my WebAssembly module.

That assumption about spawning threads directly inside a WebAssembly module is actually incorrect. You cannot create OS-level threads or use the std::thread crate within a WASM binary because it lacks native concurrency primitives and memory management for that purpose. The wasm-bindgen-rayon crate does not provide a way to spawn actual thread workers from within the compiled WASM code itself.
So I'm stuck if I want real parallelism without using a separate process? Can't wasm-bindgen-rayon just give me some kind of internal worker pool that runs inside the module?

No, wasm-bindgen-rayon does not implement an internal thread pool for WASM execution. It is designed to bridge Rust code running in a browser environment with the rayon runtime, but it relies on Web Workers from Rust as its concurrency mechanism. The crate exports worker threads that must be managed by the host JavaScript layer or a dedicated Rust sidecar process.

I see, so I need to run my Rust logic outside the WASM module? But what if I just want simple parallelism without building a whole separate server? Is there a way to get true parallel execution within a single file?

You can achieve parallelism in the browser with Web Workers from Rust by having your main thread spawn a worker process that executes a separate WASM module. The wasm-bindgen-rayon crate allows you to pass data structures like Vec or Box<dyn Any> to these workers, but it does not magically enable parallel execution inside the current binary.
Wait, so I can't just add rayon to my existing single-file WASM project and expect it to run in parallel? That's a fundamental limitation compared to what I thought wasm-bindgen-rayon offered?

Exactly. The misconception is thinking that adding the crate to your build makes native parallelism available without architectural changes. wasm-bindgen-rayon requires you to restructure your application so that heavy lifting happens in separate Web Workers from Rust processes, effectively treating each worker as a distinct execution context.

I'm trying to figure out how to run parallel tasks inside a browser environment using Rust. I have an image processing pipeline where I need to filter hundreds of thumbnails at once, but the single-threaded WASM main loop seems too slow for this workload.

That's exactly why you'd want to look into wasm-bindgen-rayon. Think of it like a multi-lane highway instead of a single lane; your threads can process chunks of thumbnails simultaneously without blocking the main event loop.
I've read about rayon but I'm not sure if the worker_threads feature actually works in browsers. Can you explain how wasm-bindgen-rayon handles this specific case?

The wasm-bindgen-rayon crate specifically implements the Worker thread abstraction for WASM, allowing your Rayon parallelism to run outside the main thread just like in Node or Linux. It's essentially a bridge that lets you use standard Rust parallel patterns inside the browser context.
So I can still use the standard rayon::parallelize method? How does this work with closures and shared data in the worker?

Yes, you can chain your Rayon parallelism just like normal Rust code. The key is that wasm-bindgen-rayon provides a special context where closures are executed in isolation, so you don't need to manually manage thread synchronization or share mutable state between the main and worker threads.
That makes sense. What about error handling if one of my parallel filters fails? Does the whole batch stop or does it just skip that item?

Rayon handles errors gracefully by collecting them into a Result vector, so your pipeline can continue processing other items even if some thumbnails fail validation. This is much better than the single-threaded approach where one error halts everything.
One last thing - does this require any special build configuration or just normal wasm-bindgen setup?

It's mostly transparent, but you need to ensure your crate is built with the --target wasm32-unknown-unknown flag and that wasm-bindgen-rayon is properly scoped in your Cargo.toml. Once configured, it behaves like a standard library call.

I've read about wasm-bindgen-rayon and how it allows parallelism in the browser, but I still don't fully understand how it connects to the web workers we already use in Node.js or what the actual trade-offs are when trying to run heavy computations like image processing on the main thread versus a worker.

The connection is that wasm-bindgen-rayon exposes Rust's Rayon parallelism library via JavaScript, allowing you to spawn Web Workers from Rust code using the Worker class. While Node.js uses native threads, browsers have restrictions where you can't simply use multiple threads on the main thread due to the single-threaded nature of V8 and WASM execution models in browsers; wasm-bindgen-rayon solves this by generating a worker script that runs your parallel logic outside the main context.

So I think what I'm missing is that I can't just drop a Rayon pool into my browser app and expect it to work like Rust's standard library, right? There must be some specific setup required beyond just importing wasm-bindgen-rayon.

You are correct in assuming you cannot simply use standard Rust parallelism directly. You need to create a Web Worker file that exports the necessary functions using wasm-bindgen, then import those into your main script. The worker runs its own WASM instance where the Rayon pool is initialized inside the worker context rather than sharing state with the main thread.

Ah, I see now. I was thinking about passing a single global Rayon pool across threads like in Rust, but actually each Web Worker gets its own isolated execution environment where it can spawn its own independent Rayon workers without blocking the main thread, which explains why we need to handle state transfer carefully.

Exactly. The key insight is that while you still use wasm-bindgen-rayon to parallelize your Rust code inside each worker, those workers are isolated processes from your browser's main JavaScript context, so you avoid the single-threaded bottleneck entirely by delegating heavy work to these separate environments.
